title: CANCELLATION: tarax SECURITY & POWER (000066526)
text:
CANCELLATION No C 66 526 (REVOCATION) 

Ecolab USA Inc., 1 Ecolab Place, 55102 St Paul, United States (applicant), represented by Haesemann & Töbelmann Partnerschaft von Rechtsanwälten mbB, Herwarthstr. 1, 50672 Köln, Germany (professional representative)

a g a i n s t

Werner & Mertz GmbH, Rheinallee 96, 55120 Mainz, Germany (EUTM proprietor), represented by Eisenführ Speiser Patentanwälte Rechtsanwälte PartGmbB, Am Kaffee-Quartier 3, 28217 Bremen, Germany (professional representative).

On 19/03/2026, the Cancellation Division takes the following

DECISION

REASONS

On 11/06/2024, the applicant filed a request for revocation of European Union trade mark No 13 272 281  (figurative mark) (the EUTM). The request is directed against all the goods covered by the EUTM, namely:

Class 3: Preparations for cleaning bathrooms and toilets.

The applicant invoked Article 58(1)(a) EUTMR.   

SUMMARY OF THE PARTIES’ ARGUMENTS

The applicant argues that the mark has not been put to genuine use for a continuous period of five years according to Article 58(1)(a) EUTMR.

The EUTM proprietor submitted evidence to demonstrate genuine use of the mark in France between 2019 and 2024. This included product images (Annex 2) and photographs of the goods available for sale in retail stores (Annex 5). To verify commercial activity, the EUTM proprietor provided a table including sales figures for the relevant period and an affidavit from I.G., head of the legal department (Annex 3), confirming the product use and the sales volumes. The EUTM proprietor also submitted copies of exemplary invoices (Annex 4) to prove that the goods were sold in France.

The EUTM proprietor explained that the products are sold by its French subsidiary, Werner & Mertz – France S.A.S.U., establishing that the use was with the proprietor's consent. Regarding the form in which the mark was used, the EUTM proprietor argued that the omission of the words ‘Security & Power’ from the registered mark does not alter its distinctive character, as these terms are merely descriptive of product characteristics. The distinctive character is preserved through the dominant elements: the word ‘TARAX’ and the red frog.

The EUTM proprietor submitted the evidence listed below.

The applicant argues that the EUTM proprietor failed to demonstrate genuine use of the contested mark during the relevant period. In particular, the EUTM proprietor's evidence fails to show a sufficient commercial volume of use. The affidavit comes from the EUTM proprietor's own legal counsel and lacks first-hand knowledge, relying on vague references to information from unnamed employees, which raises doubts about its reliability. The sales figures provided cannot be accurately assessed, as the numbers for 2019 and 2024 include periods outside the relevant timeframe. Even disregarding this, the sales numbers are very low, especially considering the substantial market for cleaning products. The invoices only show minimal sales and do not corroborate the claimed figures.


Furthermore, the mark was not used as registered. The EUTM proprietor omitted the wording ‘SECURITY & POWER’, which, according to the applicant, has a clear visual impact due to its red colour and capital letters, contributing to the mark's overall impression. Because this element was omitted and other elements (like outlines and additional words) were added, the use does not qualify as an acceptable variation not altering the distinctive character under Article 18(1)(a) EUTMR.

Finally, the evidence shows use for different goods than those covered by the registration. The applicant argues that the EUTM proprietor admits use only for ‘toilet cleaning products’, conceding non-use for ‘preparations for cleaning bathrooms’. Even if use for toilet cleaners were accepted (which is contested), it would at best be limited to a subcategory: ‘toilet cleaners for household use’. The EUTM proprietor operates a separate professional division using different brands (‘Green Care Professional’ and ‘Tana’). The ‘tarax’ products are sold exclusively in supermarkets and drugstores, confirming they are for household use only. Household and industrial toilet cleaners differ fundamentally in strength, ingredients, packaging size and distribution channels, forming distinct subcategories. Therefore, any proven use could only support this narrower subcategory. However, the applicant argues that the evidence does not prove that the contested EUTM was used for toilet cleaners at all, not even household varieties. The EUTM proprietor's description of goods for ‘cleaning drains and pipes’ and placement in the ‘sewage and cesspools’ section confirm that the goods are drain cleaners, which are distinct from toilet cleaners.

The EUTM proprietor argues that the applicant artificially creates doubts by assessing individual pieces of evidence in isolation, rather than considering the evidence as a whole. The EUTM proprietor submitted extensive evidence confirming genuine use for the registered goods.

The fact that the affidavit comes from an employee (a qualified German lawyer) does not diminish its evidentiary value. Evidence in trade mark cases naturally originates from the owner, as they are the party using the mark. The affidavit's transparency about information sources demonstrates diligence, and it was submitted under penalty of perjury.

The applicant's claim that sales numbers are low is legally irrelevant. Genuine use does not require significant market share or extensive sales. The submitted sales figures are considerable, and the invoices show relevant volumes.

Regarding use in altered form, the EUTM proprietor argues that the omission of ‘SECURITY & POWER’ does not affect the mark's distinctive character. These elements are descriptive, positioned at the bottom of the packaging and are significantly smaller than the dominant ‘tarax’ element. Omitting descriptive elements typically does not impact a sign’s distinctive character. The alleged additions (e.g. grey lines) are either present in the registered mark or are common decorative features that do not function as trade mark elements.

The EUTM proprietor demonstrated use for both categories:

	‘Preparations for cleaning bathrooms’: The ‘Deboucheurs’ (unblockers) clean bathroom sink pipes, which fall within bathroom cleaning preparations;

	‘Preparations for cleaning toilets’: The ‘tarax Hygiene WC Tablettes’ are explicitly for toilet cleaning, as confirmed by packaging and website descriptions about removing limescale and keeping toilets spotless.

The applicant's attempt to limit use to ‘household use’ is unwarranted. This artificially narrows a term that cannot be divided into clearly separable subgroups. The cited Exhibit 2 is from a biased source seeking to distinguish its products, and its language about ‘lighter tasks’ acknowledges overlapping product ranges rather than rigid categories.

‘Drain cleaners’ are covered by the broader term ‘toilet cleaners’. Moreover, the registered term ‘preparations for cleaning bathrooms and toilets’ is sufficiently broad to encompass drain cleaning, as drains are integral parts of toilets, showers and bathroom sinks. The website explicitly describes products that clean ‘from the bowl to the pipe leading to the drains’, confirming the interconnected nature of these goods.

In conclusion, the EUTM proprietor has demonstrated genuine use during the relevant period for the registered goods.

The EUTM proprietor submitted the evidence listed below.

The applicant argues that the new evidence (Annexes 6–11) is irrelevant. Annexes 9 and 10 (Amazon screenshots) are from March 2025, outside the relevant period, and do not show authorised use by the EUTM proprietor; Annex 9 refers to shoe polish, not the contested goods. Annex 8 (Wayback Machine) is entirely outside the relevant period and concerns septic maintenance and drain cleaners, not the registered goods. Overall, none of Annexes 6–11 demonstrate use of the registered goods (‘preparations for cleaning toilets and bathrooms’) or the mark in its registered form.

The evidence does not show use of the mark in its registered form or in an acceptable variation. The omission of the wording ‘SECURITY & POWER’ is material, as it has visual impact due to its red colour and is not so small as to recede in the overall impression. The addition of grey lines or shadows around ‘TARAX’ further alters the overall impression.

The EUTM proprietor acknowledged use only for ‘toilet cleaning products’, meaning there is no use for ‘preparations for cleaning bathrooms’. Even if use were proven (which it is not), it would at best be for ‘toilet cleaners for household use’. The TMClass database confirms that ‘household use’ is a valid subcategory. 

‘Drain cleaners’ are designed to dissolve blocks in pipes, while bathroom and toilet cleaners are for cleaning visible surfaces (bowls, sinks, showers). They serve different purposes, are not interchangeable and are sold in different store sections.

Products for septic tanks are not bathroom or toilet cleaners, as septic tanks are located underground outside the house.

The invoices show only low sales and do not corroborate the affidavit's figures. The 2019 and 2024 sales figures must be disregarded entirely as they include periods outside the relevant 5-year period and cannot be separated.

The EUTM proprietor rejects the applicant's arguments and maintains that genuine use for the registered goods has been sufficiently proven. 

The attacks on the affidavit are unjustified. The EUTM proprietor submitted a new affidavit as Annex 3, which now specifically identifies the sources of the information. The affidavit is sufficiently detailed and provides precise sales figures for all relevant goods.

The goods shown, including pipe and drain cleaners, fall within ‘preparations for cleaning bathrooms and toilets’. These products are specifically designed to clean the pipes and drains of bathroom sinks and toilets, making them a functional subcategory of bathroom and toilet cleaners. Since they are intended for use on bathroom and toilet pipes, they qualify as bathroom and toilet cleaners.

GROUNDS FOR THE DECISION

According to Article 58(1)(a) EUTMR, the rights of the proprietor of the European Union trade mark will be revoked on application to the Office, if, within a continuous period of five years, the trade mark has not been put to genuine use in the Union for the goods or services for which it is registered, and there are no proper reasons for non-use. 

Genuine use of a trade mark exists where the mark is used in accordance with its essential function, which is to guarantee the identity of the origin of the goods or services for which it is registered, in order to create or preserve an outlet for those goods or services. Genuine use requires actual use on the market of the registered goods and services and does not include token use for the sole purpose of preserving the rights conferred by the mark, nor use which is solely internal (11/03/2003, C‑40/01, Minimax, EU:C:2003:145, in particular § 35-37 and 43).

When assessing whether use of the trade mark is genuine, regard must be had to all the facts and circumstances relevant to establishing whether commercial exploitation of the mark is real, particularly whether such use is viewed as warranted in the economic sector concerned to maintain or create a market share for the goods or services protected by the mark (11/03/2003, C‑40/01, Minimax, EU:C:2003:145, § 38). However, the purpose of the provision requiring that the mark must have been genuinely used ‘is not to assess commercial success or to review the economic strategy of an undertaking, nor is it intended to restrict trade-mark protection to the case where large-scale commercial use has been made of the marks’ (08/07/2004, T‑203/02, VITAFRUIT / VITAFRUT, EU:T:2004:225, § 38). 

According to Article 19(1) EUTMDR in conjunction with Article 10(3) EUTMDR, the indications and evidence of use must establish the place, time, extent and nature of use of the contested trade mark for the goods and/or services for which it is registered. 

In revocation proceedings based on the grounds of non-use, the burden of proof lies with the EUTM proprietor as the applicant cannot be expected to prove a negative fact, namely that the mark has not been used during a continuous period of five years. Therefore, it is the EUTM proprietor who must prove genuine use within the European Union, or submit proper reasons for non-use.

In the present case, the EUTM was registered on 28/01/2015. The revocation request was filed on 11/06/2024. Therefore, the EUTM had been registered for more than five years at the date of the filing of the request. The EUTM proprietor had to prove genuine use of the contested EUTM during the five-year period preceding the date of the revocation request, that is, from 11/06/2019 until 10/06/2024 inclusive, for the contested goods listed in the section ‘Reasons’ above.

On 23/10/2024, 12/05/2025 and 21/11/2025 the EUTM proprietor submitted evidence as proof of use.

As the EUTM proprietor requested to keep certain commercial data contained in the evidence confidential vis-à-vis third parties, the Cancellation Division will describe the evidence only in the most general terms without divulging any such data.

The evidence to be taken into account is the following: 

	Annex 1: Index.

	Annex 2: Pictures showing the goods bearing the mark at issue.

	Annex 3: Affidavit issued by the EUTM proprietor’s Head of Legal Department, dated 22/10/2024, including a table showing sales of the following products from 2019 to 2024:

o	TARAX DEBOUCHEUR POWER GEL 5 MIN 1L, F

o	TARAX DEBOUCH GEL NON CORROSIF 1L, F

o	TARAX DEBOUCH POUDRE 3 MIN SACHET 60G, F

o	TARAX WC-TABS 8 DOSES

o	TARAX ENTRETIEN CANALISATIONS PIN, 1L, F

o	TARAX DEBOUCH. GRAN. DOS. BOT. 3MIN, 600G, F/B

o	TARAX FOSSE SEPTIQUE 6 MOIS, 200G, F

	Annex 4: Invoices issued by Werner & Mertz France S.A.S.U. to clients in France between 2019 and 2024.

	Annex 5: Undated photos of supermarket shelves showing the EUTM proprietor’s products.

	Annexes 6–7: Excerpts from the website www.tarax.fr showing the goods sold under the mark at issue, obtained through the Wayback Machine, dated between 11/05/2021 and 02/04/2023.

	Annex 8: Excerpts from the domain tarax.fr, obtained via the Wayback Machine, dated 11/05/2021, showing the history of the mark through a timeline.

	Annexes 9–10: German and French Amazon pages showing the EUTM proprietor’s products, which have been offered on Amazon since 28/03/2021.

	Annex 11: Picture of the packaging for the product ‘TARAX Entretien fosses’, 1 maxi sachet, providing 6 months of protection.

	Annex 12: Affidavit issued by the EUTM proprietor’s Head of Legal Department on 20/11/2025, which, as claimed by the EUTM proprietor, is identical in content to the one submitted as Annex 3 but additionally specifies the sources from which the information was obtained. 

Admissibility of belated evidence

On 12/05/2025 and 21/11/2025, after expiry of the time limit, the EUTM proprietor submitted additional evidence. 

Even though, according to Article 19(1) EUTMDR, the EUTM proprietor has to submit proof of use within a time limit set by the Office Article 10(7) EUTMDR (applicable to cancellation proceeding by virtue of Article 19(1) EUTMDR) expressly invites the Office to exercise its discretionary power if relevant evidence was submitted in time and, after the expiry of the time limit, supplementary evidence was filed.

According to Article 10(7) EUTMDR, where, after the expiry of the time limit set by the Office indications or evidence is filed that supplement prior relevant indications or evidence submitted within the time limit, the Office may take into account the evidence submitted out of time as a result of exercise of the discretion conferred on it by Article 95(2) EUTMR. When exercising its discretionary power, the Office must take into account, in particular, the stage of proceedings and whether the facts or evidence are, prima facie, likely to be relevant for the outcome of the case and whether there are valid reasons for the late submission of the facts or evidence.

In this regard, the Cancellation Division considers that the EUTM proprietor did submit relevant evidence within the time limit initially set by the Office and, therefore, the later evidence can be considered to be additional.

The fact that the applicant disputed the initial evidence submitted by the EUTM proprietor justifies the submission of additional evidence in reply to the objection (29/09/2011, T‑415/09, FISHBONE / FISHBONE Beachwear (fig.), EU:T:2011:550, § 30 and 33, upheld by judgment of 18/07/2013, C‑621/11 P, FISHBONE / FISHBONE Beachwear (fig.), EU:C:2013:484,  § 36).

As regards the evidence submitted on 21/11/2025, it merely serves as a confirmation of the evidence already submitted. The applicant has not had the opportunity to comment on this evidence. However, as it does not introduce any new elements, but merely reinforces what was already submitted within the prescribed time limit, the Cancellation Division does not consider it necessary to reopen the proceedings for a further round of observations regarding this specific document. 

For the above reasons, and in the exercise of its discretion pursuant to Article 95(2) EUTMR, the Cancellation Division therefore decides to take into account the additional evidence.

PRELIMINARY REMARKS 

As far as the affidavit is concerned, Article 10(4) EUTMDR (applicable to cancellation proceedings by virtue of Article 19(1) EUTMDR) expressly mentions written statements referred to in Article 97(1)(f) EUTMR as admissible means of proof of use. Article 97(1)(f) EUTMR lists, as means of giving evidence, sworn or affirmed written statements or other statements that have a similar effect under the law of the State in which they were drawn up. As far as the probative value of this kind of evidence is concerned, statements drawn up by the interested parties themselves or their employees are generally given less weight than independent evidence. This is because the perceptions of a party involved in a dispute may be more or less affected by its personal interests in the matter.

However, this does not mean that such statements do not have any probative value at all.

The final outcome depends on the overall assessment of the evidence in the particular case. The probative value of such statements depends on whether or not they are supported by other types of evidence (labels, packaging etc.) or evidence originating from independent sources.

In view of the foregoing, the remaining evidence must be assessed in order to see whether or not the contents of the declaration are supported by the other items of evidence.

ASSESSMENT OF GENUINE USE – FACTORS

Time of use

The evidence must show genuine use of the contested EUTM within the relevant period.

The evidence submitted is dated within the relevant period, namely from 11/06/2019 to 10/06/2024 inclusive. While the sales figures for 2019 and 2024 contained in the affidavit include some months falling outside the relevant period, as noted by the applicant, this does not prevent this evidence from being taken into account, as it helps assess use within the relevant timeframe. The invoices provide direct proof of sales transactions within the relevant period.

Therefore, the evidence of use filed by the EUTM proprietor contains sufficient indications concerning the time of use.

Place of use

The evidence must show that the contested EUTM has been genuinely used in the European Union (see Article 18(1) EUTMR and Article 58(1)(a) EUTMR).

The evidence, in particular invoices, shows that the place of use is France. This can be inferred from the language of the documents and some addresses in this country. 

Therefore, the evidence of use filed by the EUTM proprietor contains sufficient indications concerning the place of use.

Nature of use: use as a trade mark

Nature of use requires, inter alia, that the contested EUTM is used as a trade mark, that is, for identifying origin, thus making it possible for the relevant public to distinguish between goods and services of different providers.

In the present case, the EUTM was used as a trade mark. The evidence submitted shows that the contested sign was affixed to the packaging of the goods. Consequently, there is a sufficient link to prove that the goods concerned are provided under the contested mark.

Nature of use: use of the mark as registered

‘Nature of use’ in the context of Article 10(3) EUTMDR further requires evidence of use of the mark as registered, or of a variation thereof which, pursuant to Article 18(1)(a) EUTMR, does not alter the distinctive character of the contested EUTM. 

The purpose of Article 18(1)(a) EUTMR is to avoid imposing strict conformity between the form of the trade mark used and the form in which the mark was registered. The proprietor of the mark is allowed to use variations of the sign in its commercial exploitation that, without altering its distinctive character, enable it to be better adapted to the marketing and promotion requirements of the goods or services concerned. 

Assessment of the distinctive or dominant character of one or more components of a complex trade mark must be based on the intrinsic qualities of each of those components, as well as on the relative position of the different components within the trade mark (24/11/2005, T‑135/04, Online Bus, EU:T:2005:419, § 36).

The evidence shows that the mark has been used as, for example, ‘TARAX WC-TABS 8 DOSES’ and .

In the registered sign, the word ‘tarax’ and the red frog device are inherently distinctive. The wording ‘SECURITY & POWER’ is descriptive of product characteristics, namely safety and cleaning efficacy, and is positioned subordinate at the bottom of the mark in smaller size.

The mark as used omits the wording ‘SECURITY & POWER’, while it retains ‘tarax’ (sometimes with grey outlines or shadows) and the red frog device. It also includes additional decorative elements, such as lines, and descriptive indications, like ‘HYGIENE’ (indicating the product’s sanitary function) and ‘WC-TABS 8 DOSES’ (specifying the type and quantity of the toilet-cleaning tablets).

The omission of ‘SECURITY & POWER’ does not affect the mark’s distinctive character, as it is descriptive and occupies a subordinate position. Likewise, the addition of non-distinctive or weakly distinctive elements—decorative lines or shadows or terms such as ‘HYGIENE’ or ‘WC-TABS 8 DOSES’—does not alter the mark’s distinctive character.

Accordingly, the mark as used retains its distinctive character. The omission of descriptive wording and the addition of non-distinctive elements do not alter the distinctive character of the mark. The mark as used is broadly equivalent to the mark as registered.

Extent of use

Concerning extent of use, it is settled case-law that account must be taken, in particular, of the commercial volume of the overall use, as well as of the length of the period during which the mark was used and the frequency of use (e.g. 08/07/2004, T-334/01, Hipoviton / HIPPOVIT, EU:T:2004:223, § 35).

The Court has held that ‘use of the mark need not [...] always be quantitatively significant for it to be deemed genuine, as that depends on the characteristics of the goods or service concerned on the corresponding market’ (11/03/2003, C-40/01, Minimax, EU:C:2003:145, § 39). 

It is not possible to determine a priori, and in the abstract, what quantitative threshold should be chosen in order to determine whether use is genuine or not. A de minimis rule cannot therefore be laid down. When it serves a real commercial purpose, even minimal use of the mark can be sufficient to establish genuine use (27/01/2004, C-259/02, Laboratoire de la mer, EU:C:2004:50, § 25, 27).

The assessment of genuine use entails a degree of interdependence between the factors taken into account. Thus, the fact that commercial volume achieved under the mark was not high may be offset by the fact that use of the mark was extensive or very regular, and vice versa. Likewise, the territorial scope of the use is only one of several factors to be taken into account, so that a limited territorial scope of use can be counteracted by a more significant volume or duration of use.

The submitted invoices show actual commercial transactions, not merely internal use, throughout the relevant period, including sales to retailers such as Auchan. This clearly goes beyond token use and demonstrates a real commercial effort to maintain a market presence.

The applicant argues that the sales figures are low and therefore cannot constitute genuine use of the mark. This argument is unfounded. The assessment of genuine use is not intended to evaluate the commercial success of a product or to review the economic strategy of an undertaking, nor is it limited to cases where large-scale commercial use has been made of the mark (26/09/2013, C-609/11 P, Centrotherm, EU:C:2013:1449, §§ 72, 74; 29/11/2018, C-340/17P, ALCOLOCK, EU:C:2018:965, § 90; 02/02/2016, T-171/13, MOTOBI B PESARO, EU:T:2016:54, § 49). Use of a mark need not be quantitatively significant for it to be deemed genuine. The EUTM proprietor is not required to prove specific quantities sold.

What must be shown is that the EU market forms part of the EUTM proprietor’s commercial strategy, resulting in actual trade. That trade need not be high, but it must be real and demonstrable, rather than token use intended solely to preserve trade mark rights. The key factor in assessing genuine use is whether the undertaking has sought to create or maintain a commercial outlet for its goods.

Furthermore, case-law confirms that an accumulation of evidence may suffice to establish genuine use, even if individual items of evidence would be insufficient on their own (17/04/2008, C-108/07 P, FERRO/FERRERO, EU:C:2008:234, § 36; 24/05/2012, T-152/11, MAD (fig.), EU:T:2012:263, § 34).

Taken as a whole, the evidence is sufficient to prove that the commercial exploitation of the mark is real and not merely token.

Use in relation to the registered goods 

Article 58(1)(a) EUTMR and Article 10(3) EUTMDR require that the EUTM proprietor proves genuine use for the contested goods and services for which the European Union trade mark is registered.

The contested EUTM is registered for ‘preparations for cleaning bathrooms and toilets’ in Class 3.

The registered specification constitutes a single, coherent category of goods aimed at maintaining hygiene in the sanitary environment of a bathroom. 

The evidence shows that the ‘tarax Hygiene WC Tablettes’ is a preparation designed for cleaning and maintaining a core fixture bathroom fixture, namely the toilet. The product packaging and the website description confirm that the tablets are placed in the toilet bowl to remove limescale and to keep toilets ‘clean and fresh’ and ‘spotless’.

The product’s purpose, namely cleaning and deodorising the toilet bowl, is an integral part of overall bathroom hygiene. A consumer seeking to clean their bathroom would naturally consider cleaning the toilet as part of that task.

To argue that a product specifically designed to clean a toilet (a fixture permanently installed in a bathroom) does not constitute a ‘preparation for cleaning bathrooms’ would require an artificially narrow interpretation, excluding toilets from the definition of a bathroom. This contradicts both commercial reality and consumer perception. 

The ‘tarax Hygiene WC Tablettes’ is therefore a preparation for cleaning an essential component of the bathroom. Consequently, use of the mark for this product constitutes genuine use for the registered specification of ‘preparations for cleaning bathrooms and toilets’. 

The applicant argues that use should be limited to products for ‘household use’. However, the essential purpose of the goods—cleaning bathroom and toilet fixtures—remains the same whether they are used in a home or a commercial setting. Differences in concentration, packaging size or distribution channels do not change the fundamental nature of the goods for the purpose of defining the scope of protection. They remain, at their core, ‘preparations for cleaning bathrooms and toilets’.

Moreover, the fact that that the EUTM proprietor uses separate brands—'Green Care Professional' and 'Tana'—for its industrial cleaning products does not justify limiting the 'tarax' registration to household use. This commercial practice merely reflects a marketing strategy, whereby different brands are used for different market segments. It does not imply that 'tarax' could not be used for professional purposes, nor does it legally restrict the scope of protection conferred by the registration. The EUTM proprietor is entitled to maintain its registration for the entire category of goods covered. The existence of other brands for industrial products does not narrow the protection for 'tarax'.

Finally, the applicant's reference to TMClass entries showing goods with the indication 'for household use' does not determine the scope of protection of the contested registration. The specification is clear and unambiguous, and there is no basis to narrow it. 

Overall assessment

In order to examine, in a given case, whether use of the mark is genuine, an overall assessment must be made taking account of all the relevant factors in the particular case. That assessment implies a certain interdependence between the factors taken into account. Thus, a low volume of goods marketed under that trade mark may be compensated for by high intensity of use or a certain constancy regarding the time of use of that trade mark or vice versa (08/07/2004, T-334/01, Hipoviton / HIPPOVIT, EU:T:2004:223, § 36).

In the present case, the Cancellation Division considers that genuine use of the contested mark has been sufficiently demonstrated for the relevant factors in relation to all the goods.

Conclusion

It follows from the above that the EUTM proprietor has proven genuine use for all the contested goods. Consequently, the application for revocation must be rejected as unfounded.

COSTS

According to Article 109(1) EUTMR, the losing party in cancellation proceedings must bear the fees and costs incurred by the other party.

Since the applicant is the losing party, it must bear the costs incurred by the EUTM proprietor in the course of these proceedings.

According to Article 109(7) EUTMR and Article 18(1)(c)(ii) EUTMIR, the costs to be paid to the EUTM proprietor are the representation costs, which are to be fixed on the basis of the maximum rate set therein. 

The Cancellation Division

According to Article 67 EUTMR, any party adversely affected by this decision has a right to appeal against this decision. According to Article 68 EUTMR, notice of appeal must be filed in writing at the Office within two months of the date of notification of this decision. It must be filed in the language of the proceedings in which the decision subject to appeal was taken. Furthermore, a written statement of the grounds of appeal must be filed within four months of the same date. The notice of appeal will be deemed to be filed only when the appeal fee of EUR 720 has been paid.
